title: Towards Scalable Quaternary Message-Passing Decoding for Quantum Error Correction
authors: Boqing Zhang, Henry D. Pfister, Hanwen Yao, Siyuan Niu
abstract: The scalability and interpretability of message-passing (MP) decoding, such as (quaternary) Belief Propagation, remain open challenges in quantum error correction. Even for surface codes, arguably the first testbed for decoding methods, studies of improved MP decoders have mostly been restricted to small distances ($d \lesssim 19$). Moreover, the mismatch with established message-passing theory limits the decoder's interpretability, making it unclear whether MP decoding can sustain its effectiveness at large system sizes. This work takes a step toward a more principled and interpretable MP decoding framework, with the goal of making MP-based decoding more reliable and bridging theory and practice. We introduce a dilution method, which allows a quaternary Min-Sum (MS) decoder to exhibit an apparent depolarizing threshold of $16\%$ up to distance $20$, outperforming Minimum-Weight Perfect Matching in finite-length regimes. Notably, for $X$-noise, the standard MS decoder under dilution has worst-case complexity $O(N \log^2 d)$ and outperforms BP-OSD at $d=65$. The observed $\sim 9\%$ threshold may correspond to a true asymptotic threshold. Finally, we give a graph-dilution argument that interprets the success of the dilution method and offers insight into when MP algorithms can genuinely scale. Taken together, these results provide encouraging progress toward scalable and interpretable MP decoding in quantum error correction.
summary: This paper proposes a new decoding strategy called the 'dilution method' to make message-passing algorithms more effective for large-scale quantum error correction. By iteratively sparsifying the decoding graph and freezing variables, the authors demonstrate that decoders can overcome the traditional failures caused by short cycles in quantum codes, achieving high error thresholds even at large distances.
main_problem: Addressing the scalability and interpretability challenges of message-passing decoders in quantum error correction, specifically the non-convergence caused by short cycles and high degeneracy in stabilizer codes.
main_result: The introduction of a 'dilution method' enables a quaternary Min-Sum decoder to achieve an apparent depolarizing threshold of 16% and a sustainable threshold of ~9% for X-noise at large distances (d=65), outperforming MWPM and BP-OSD.
method: A multi-stage decoding framework using a sequence of progressively sparsified (diluted) Tanner graphs combined with a global hard-decimation step to freeze qubit variables.
model_or_system: Quantum error correction using CSS and surface codes, specifically focusing on the joint X-Z Tanner graph under depolarizing and biased (X-only) Pauli noise models.
key_observables: Logical error rate, decoding threshold (p_th), cavity discrepancy (delta), and decoding complexity.
important_parameters: Code distance (d), sparsification ratio (s), damping factor (epsilon), and X-Z error coupling (kappa).
main_assumptions: Assumes that auxiliary non-MP steps incur near-constant overhead and utilizes a heuristic non-expansion assumption for error weight growth across dilution stages.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Tanner graph decomposition; Figures 2-4 illustrate different sparsification patterns (diagonal and Cartesian); Figures 8-9 show performance/thresholds under different noise models; Figures 10-15 detail complexity, convergence, and the theoretical impact of cycles and error weight expansion.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the quaternary message-passing framework for CSS codes, introduces the graph-dilution/sparsification method, provides theoretical bounds on error-correcting radii and complexity, analyzes the impact of X-Z coupling on convergence via cavity discrepancy, and concludes with numerical evidence of scalable thresholds.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Illustration of the canonical Tanner graph T of the distance-5 surface code (left) and the corresponding component lattices GX (blue) and GZ (red) (right). In T , black variable nodes represent physical qubits, blue and red factor nodes correspond to X- and Z-type stabilizers, respectively. This Tanner graph decomposes into two canonical lattices in which qubits lie on edges and blue (resp. red) vertices represent X-type (resp. Z-type) stabilizers.

Figure 2. Illustration of quaternary message passing for CSS codes. The left panel shows the Z- (resp. X-) factor- to-variable update ˆν(t) aZ→i(xi) (resp. ˆµ(t) aX→i(zi) ) from aZ (resp. aX) to variable node i, which proceeds independently

Figure 3. Examples of diluted graphs obtained by diagonal s-sparsification. From left to right: the D1 V -diluted lattice G1 DV , the D1 H-diluted lattice G1 DH , and the D3 V -diluted lattice G3 DV .

Figure 4. Examples of diluted graphs obtained by Cartesian s-sparsification. From left to right: the C1 V -diluted lattice G1 CV , the C1 H-diluted lattice G1 CH , and the C3 V -diluted lattice G3 CV .

Figure 5. Illustration of effective errors on diluted lattices. The middle panels show physical errors (red strings) on the original lattice, and their corresponding effective errors on the diluted lattices are shown in purple. The effective errors on the right are stabilizer-equivalent to the physical error, while the one on the left differs by a logical operator.
